questions.question: What is the period of the function y = sin(2x)?
questions.explanation: For y = sin(bx), the period is 2π/b. Here b = 2, so period = 2π/2 = π.

questions.question: In triangle ABC, angle A = 40°, angle B = 70°, and side a = 10. Using the Law of Sines, find the length of side b to the nearest tenth.
questions.explanation: By Law of Sines: a/sin(A) = b/sin(B). So 10/sin(40°) = b/sin(70°). Solving: b = 10·sin(70°)/sin(40°) ≈ 10(0.9397)/(0.6428) ≈ 14.1.

questions.question: What is the amplitude of y = 3cos(x) + 2?
questions.explanation: Amplitude is the absolute value of the coefficient in front of the trig function. Here that coefficient is 3. The +2 is a vertical shift, not part of amplitude.

questions.question: In triangle PQR, p = 5, q = 7, and angle R = 60°. Using the Law of Cosines, find r.
At what x-value does y = sin(x) reach its maximum on the interval [0, 2π]?
questions.explanation: The sine function reaches its maximum value of 1 at x = π/2 within one period [0, 2π].

questions.question: In triangle ABC, a = 8, b = 6, and c = 5. Find angle C using the Law of Cosines. Round to the nearest degree.
questions.explanation: By Law of Cosines: c² = a² + b² - 2ab·cos(C). So 25 = 64 + 36 - 2(8)(6)·cos(C). Thus 25 = 100 - 96·cos(C), giving cos(C) = 75/96 ≈ 0.7813. Therefore C ≈ 39°.
